# Summary of Data Analysis

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Analysis Summary

In this project, I analyzed whether students who frequently create their own examples while learning (`own_examples`) tend to have higher understanding of course material (`understanding`). I used survey data from COMP110 students and filtered relevant columns to compare these variables. To explore this relationship, I used data transformation functions (`columnar`, `select`, and `convert_columns_to_int`) and created visualizations using Seaborn.


## Visualizations
<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/scatter_plot.png" alt="Image of Scatter Plot. "  width="500"/>

<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/box_plot.png" alt="Image of Box Plot. "  width="500"/>

<img src="<custom-path>/static/imgs/line_chart.png" alt="Image of Line Chart. "  width="500"/>


## Final Conclusions

Based on my analysis, there appears to be a positive relationship between students who frequently create their own examples and their level of understanding. Students who reported higher usage of self-generated examples tended to show higher understanding scores in both scatterplot and boxplot visualizations.

Overall, this supports the idea that encouraging students to create their own examples may improve learning outcomes. However, this approach may increase workload and should be designed carefully to avoid overwhelming students.
